blocks.text: Pulmonary Targeting with Biodegradable Salbutamol-Loaded Nanoparticles Moritz Beck-Broichsitter,1,2 Julia Gauss,1 Tobias Gessler, Ph.D.,1 Werner Seeger, M.D.,1 Thomas Kissel, Ph.D.,2 and Thomas Schmehl, Ph.D.1 Abstract Background: Aerosol therapy using particulate drug carriers has become an increasingly attractive method to deliver therapeutic or diagnostic compounds to the lung. Polymeric nanoparticles are widely investigated car- riers in nanomedicine. The targeted and controlled release of drugs from nanoparticles for pulmonary delivery, however, is a research ﬁeld that has been so far rather unexploited. Therefore, the objective of this study was to compare the pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of salbutamol after aerosolization as solution or entrapped into novel polymeric nanoparticles in an isolated rabbit lung model (IPL). Methods: Physicochemical properties, morphology, encapsulation efﬁciency, in vitro drug release, stability of nanoparticles to nebulization, as well as pulmonary drug absorption and distribution after nebulization in the IPL were investigated. Results: Salbutamol-loaded poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) and poly(vinyl sulfonate-co-vinyl alcohol)- graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (VS(72)-10) nanoparticles were prepared by a modiﬁed solvent displace- ment technique with a mean particle size of approximately 120 nm and a polydispersity index below 0.150. VS(72)-10 nanoparticles showed a more negative z-potential of 54.2  3.3 mV compared to PLGA nanoparticles (36.5  2.6 mV). Salbutamol encapsulation efﬁciency was 25.2  4.9% and 63.4  3.5% for PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles, respectively. After nebulization utilizing the MicroSprayer physicochemical properties of salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles were virtually unchanged, whereas nebulized salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles showed a signiﬁcant increase in mean particle size and polydispersity. In vitro release studies demonstrated a sustained release of the encapsulated salbutamol from VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. In parallel, a sustained salbutamol release proﬁle was observed after aerosol delivery of these particles to the IPL as reﬂected by a lower salbutamol recovery in the perfusate (40.2  5.8%) when compared to PLGA nanoparticles (55.2  9.1%) and salbutamol solution (62.8  7.1%). Conclusions: The current study suggests that inhalative delivery of biodegradable nanoparticles may be a viable approach for the treatment of respiratory diseases. Key words: nanoparticles; salbutamol; biodegradable branched polyesters; nebulization; isolated lung; pulmo- nary drug delivery; targeting; sustained release Introduction P ulmonary drug delivery has become a well-established approach in the treatment of local respiratory diseases, and provides an excellent example of targeted drug therapy. Targeted drug delivery to the lungs is attractive for several reasons. With the direct delivery of therapeutic agents to the desired site of action, rapid onset of drug action, lower sys- temic exposure, and consequently, reduced side effects can be achieved. A signiﬁcant disadvantage of inhalation therapy is the relatively short duration of the pharmacological effects demanding multiple daily inhalation maneuvers.(1) Strategies for further improvement of inhalation ther- apy include development of pulmonary controlled release 1Medical Clinic II, Department of Internal Medicine, Justus-Liebig-University Giessen, Klinikstrasse 36, D-35392 Giessen, Germany. 2Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Philipps-University, Marburg, Ketzerbach 63, D-35037 Marburg, Germany. JOURNAL OF AEROSOL MEDICINE AND PULMONARY DRUG DELIVERY Volume 23, Number 1, 2010 ª Mary Ann Liebert, Inc. Pp. 47–57 DOI: 10.1089=jamp.2009.0759 47

formulations, for example, microparticles.(2) In recent years nanomedicine has become an attractive concept for the tar- geted delivery of therapeutic(3) and diagnostic(4) compounds to the lung. Among the various drug delivery systems con- sidered for pulmonary application the use of biodegradable polymeric nanoparticles represents several advantages for the treatment of respiratory diseases. A number of different strategies have been proposed for modiﬁcation of nano- particle characteristics to control their behavior within bio- logical environments, like cell-speciﬁc targeted drug delivery or modiﬁed biological distribution of drugs, both at the cel- lular and organ level.(5,6) They hold great promise to over- come the stringent requirements placed on these delivery systems, such as ability to be transferred into an aerosol, protection of drugs against degradation, biocompatibility, targeting of speciﬁc sites or cell populations in the lung, and release of the drug in a predetermined manner.(7) In addition to biocompatibility, the degradability of the polymeric drug carriers within an acceptable period of time must also be taken into account. Slowly or nondegrading polymers may lead to accumulation in the lung when repeated application is nec- essary. For the above-mentioned reasons, most common biodegradable polymers with prolonged biodegradation rates such as poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA), are not suitable for application in the respiratory tract. One way to overcome this problem is to synthesize polymers with faster degradation. Fast degrading polymers, composed of short PLGA chains grafted onto charge-modiﬁed poly(vinyl alco- hol) (PVA) backbones, have been designed for drug delivery. The adjustable properties of these branched polyesters make them highly suitable for pulmonary formulations.(8–10) Wang et al.(11) have recently synthesized a new class of negatively charged branched polyesters, namely, poly(vinyl sulfonate- co-vinyl alcohol)-graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide), abbre- viated as VS, suitable for nanoparticle production. The introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure would promote electrostatic interac- tions with oppositely charged drugs, like salbutamol, thereby improving the design of nanoparticulate carriers as drug de- livery systems to the lung. Different methods are used to assess the behavior of drug- loaded pulmonary delivery systems ranging from in vitro to in vivo systems.(12) Ex vivo isolated, perfused, and ventilated lungs are commonly used models to investigate the phar- macokinetics of inhaled drugs.(13–15) In ex vivo lung models, lung-speciﬁc pharmacokinetic effects, like drug absorption and distribution proﬁles, can be investigated without the contribution of systemic absorption, distribution, and elimi- nation of the drug. Currently a large number of approved therapeutic agents are on the market for treatment of respiratory diseases. However, no sustained release formulation for pulmonary drug delivery has been marketed so far, despite the in- creasing interest and progress in pharmaceutical research. The aim of the current study was to compare the pulmo- nary absorption and distribution characteristics of the hy- drophilic drug salbutamol, a commonly used short-acting b2-agonist for symptom management of asthma, after aero- solization as either solution or entrapped into two different nanoparticle formulations in an isolated, perfused, and ventilated rabbit lung model (IPL). The preparation method of nanoparticles made of a linear polyester (PLGA) and a promising new class of biocompatible, branched polyesters (VS), respectively, loaded with salbutamol by a modiﬁed solvent displacement technique is described. The particle size, polydispersity index, z-potential, particle morphology, drug encapsulation efﬁciency, and in vitro drug release of the prepared salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle formulations were investigated using photon correlation spectroscopy (PCS), laser Doppler anemometry (LDA), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Furthermore, the stability of the nanoparticles to the nebulization procedure utilizing a MicroSprayer was de- termined using the above-mentioned techniques. The aerosol characteristics of all formulations were analyzed by laser light scattering. Finally, the pulmonary drug absorption proﬁles for the three formulations after nebulization in an IPL were compared by monitoring the salbutamol concen- tration in the perfusate with time. Additionally, drug dis- tribution in the different compartments of the IPL was evaluated at the end of the experiments. Materials and Methods Materials The biocompatible, linear polyester, namely, poly(D,L- lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA), Resomer RG 502 H was ac- quired from Boehringer Ingelheim (Ingelheim, Germany). The biocompatible, branched polyester, namely, poly(vinyl sulfonate-co-vinyl alcohol)-graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide), was synthesized and characterized as described previ- ously.(11) These branched polyesters were speciﬁcally de- signed for drug delivery to the lung and are comprised of short PLGA chains grafted onto a sulfonate substituted PVA backbone. As abbreviation A(x)-y is used where A decribes the type of backbone substitution (VS ¼ vinyl sulfonate), x the total average number of sulfonate functions on the PVA backbone, and y the PLGA side-chain length. In this study VS(72)-10 was employed. The molecular structures of both polymers used as nanoparticle matrix material are depicted in Figure 1. The b2-agonist salbutamol (free base) was a gift from Boehringer Ingelheim. All other chemicals and solvents used in this study were of the highest analytical grade com- mercially available. Methods Preparation of salbutamol solution. The drug solution containing salbutamol was prepared in isotonic phosphate- buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 to yield a ﬁnal concentration of 500 mg=mL. Preparation of nanoparticles. Salbutamol-loaded nano- particles were prepared by a modiﬁed solvent displacement technique.(16) To avoid artiﬁcial surfactants, the preparation was modiﬁed in the following manner: polymer (47.5 mg) and drug (2.5 mg) were dissolved together in 1 mL of acetone at 258C. The resulting solution was injected into a magneti- cally stirred (500 rpm) aqueous phase of 5 mL of ﬁltrated and double-distilled water (pH 7.0, conductance 0.055 mS=cm, 258C) using a special apparatus. The apparatus consists of an electronically adjustable single-suction pump, which was used to inject the organic solution into the aqueous phase through an injection needle (Fine-Ject 0.630 mm) at a 48 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

constant ﬂow rate (10.0 mL=min). The pump rate was regu- lated and constantly monitored by electric power control. After injection of the organic phase, the resulting colloidal suspension was stirred for *3 h under reduced pressure to remove the organic solvent. Particles were characterized and used directly after preparation. Physicochemical characterization of nanoparticles Particle size measurement. The particle size distribution of the obtained nanoparticles was determined by PCS using a Zetasizer NanoZS=ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments, Herren- berg, Germany). The analysis was performed at a tem- perature of 258C using samples appropriately diluted with ﬁltrated and double-distilled water to avoid multiscattering events. The DTS V. 5.10-software was used to calculate particle mean diameter (Z-Ave) and the width of the ﬁtted Gaussian distribution, which is displayed as the polydis- persity index (PDI). Each size measurement was performed with at least 10 runs. All measurements were carried out in triplicate directly after nanoparticle preparation. z-potential measurement. The z-potential was measured by LDA using a Zetasizer NanoZS=ZEN3600 (Malvern In- struments). The analysis was performed at a temperature of 258C using samples appropriately diluted with 1.56 mM NaCl solution to maintain a constant ionic strength. The DTS V. 5.10-software was used to calculate the average z-potential values from the data of multiple runs. All measurements were carried out in triplicate directly after nanoparticle preparation. AFM Morphology of nanoparticles was analyzed by AFM. Samples were prepared by placing a sample volume of 10 mL onto commercial glass slides. The slides were incubated with the nanoparticle suspension for 10 min before washing twice with distilled water and drying in a stream of dry nitrogen. Samples were investigated within 2 h after preparation. AFM was performed using a NanoWizard (JPK Instruments, Berlin, Germany) in intermittent contact mode to avoid damage to the sample surface. Commercially available Si3N4 tips attached to I-type cantilevers with a length of 230 mm and a nominal force constant of 40 N=m (NSC16 AlBS, Micromasch, Tallinn, Estonia) were used. The scan fre- quency was between 0.5 and 1 Hz and antiproportional to the scan size. The results were visualized as trace signal in amplitude mode. Determination of entrapped salbutamol To determine encapsulation efﬁciency samples of nano- particle suspensions (175 mL) were subjected to ultracentri- fugation (Airfuge, Beckman Coulter, Krefeld, Germany) at 110,000 rpm (199,000g) for 15 min at 48C. After centrifuga- tion the supernatant was carefully removed and sampled to determine the amount of unencapsulated salbutamol. The remaining pellets were freeze dried (Beta II, Christ, Osterode, Germany), weighed, and then dissolved in chloroform. After complete dissolution acidic water (adjusted to pH 2.5 with phosphoric acid) was added to the chloroform phase to ex- tract salbutamol. The two phases were mixed vigorously for 1 h and then centrifuged for 5 min at 16,000g (Centrifuge 5418, Eppendorf, Hamburg, Germany) to achieve phase sep- aration. A sample was taken from the aqueous phase to determine the amount of encapsulated salbutamol. Salbuta- mol concentrations were determined by HPLC as described below. In vitro release studies The in vitro drug release studies were carried out in PBS pH 7.4 over a 240-min period of time, according to the run time of the experiments with the IPL model. The studies were performed with nanoparticle suspensions with a theo- retical salbutamol content of 500 mg=mL. Samples of the na- noparticle suspensions (1 mL) were transferred to plastic tubes and diluted with the release medium resulting in a volume of 3 mL. Incubation occurred at 378C while under- going vertical rotation (20 rpm) (Rotatherm, Gebr. Liebisch, Bielefeld, Germany). Samples of 175 mL were taken at pre- determined time points and centrifuged as described above prior to determining the cumulative release of salbutamol by HPLC. In parallel, salbutamol was incubated in the release medium under the same conditions to exclude loss of sal- butamol during incubation time. IPL model The IPL model has been previously described.(17) Brieﬂy, male rabbits weighing 2.5 to 3.5 kg were gradually anesthe- tized with a mixture of ketamine (Ketavet, Pharmacia, Erlangen, Germany) and xylazine (Rompun, Bayer Vital, Leverkusen, Germany) in a ratio of 3 to 2 and anticoagulated with heparin (1000 U=kg). After achieving a deep anestheti- zation the animals were ventilated with room air via a tracheal cannula using a Harvard respirator (cat=rabbit Ventilator, Hugo Sachs Elektronik, March Hugstetten, Germany). FIG. 1. Structures of the polymers used in this study: (a) PLGA and (b) VS(72)-10. PULMONARY TARGETING WITH SALBUTAMOL-LOADED NANOPARTICLES 49

Catheters were inserted into the left atrium and the pul- monary artery after a midsternal thoracotomy. Perfusion with ice-cold Krebs-Henseleit buffer (Serag-Wiessner, Naila= Bayern, Germany) with a pulsatile ﬂow of 10–20 mL=min was immediately started. The lungs were removed from the thorax and placed in a temperature equilibrated chamber at 48C, freely suspended from a force transducer for monitoring the lung weight. Then the perfusion ﬂow was slowly increased to 100 mL=min. At the same time the temperature of the perfu- sion ﬂuid and the housing chamber was raised to 398C. After a 30-min steady-state period, the perfusion ﬂuid was exchanged once by fresh buffer (total volume 345 mL). With the onset of artiﬁcial perfusion an air mixture of 21% O2, 5.3% CO2, and 73.7% N2 was used for ventilation (tidal volume 30 mL, fre- quency 30 strokes=min). A positive end-expiratory pressure of 1 cm H2O was used throughout. Pulmonary arterial pressure (PAP), pulmonary venous pressure (PVP, measured in the left atrium), inﬂation pressure, and the weight of the isolated lung were registered continuously. Left atrial pressure was set 1.5 mmHg in all experiments. Only those lungs were selected for the study that fulﬁlled three criteria: ﬁrst, homogeneous white appearance with no signs of hemostasis, edema, or at- electasis; second, a pulmonary artery and ventilation pres- sure in the normal range; and third, no weight gain during the steady-state period. Sampling, evaporation and ﬂuid dripping caused losses of perfusate during the experiments (in total *55 mL=240 min). The perfusate drops that escaped from the surface of the lungs (*4 mL=240 min) were collected and analyzed. Nanoparticle stability during nebulization The stability of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles to neb- ulization with the MicroSprayer (Model IA-1B, Penn- Century, Philadelphia, PA) was investigated prior to the IPL experiments. The device is based on a stainless steel tube (19-gauge) and is operated by a plastic syringe. The aerosol is generated by an atomizer at the very tip of the tube (diameter: 1.5 mm), thus permitting to deliver aerosols directly into the lung. Brieﬂy, samples of 1-mL suspension were aerosolized to exhaustive nebulization of the reservoir. The nebulized aerosol was collected in a 15 mL tube for further analysis. The stability of the nebulized nanoparticle suspensions was investigated using PCS, LDA, and AFM. In addition, the amount of encapsulated salbutamol of the aerosolized nanoparticles was measured as described above. MicroSprayer characterization Aerosol particle sizes of NaCl 0.9% (m=v), salbutamol solution, salbutamol-loaded PLGA, and VS(72)-10 nano- particle suspensions were determined using laser light scat- tering (HELOS, Sympatec, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany). All data were analyzed in the Mie mode. The mass median aerodynamic diameters (MMAD) of the aerosol droplets generated from the MicroSprayer were in the range of 27 to 32 mm for all nebulized formulations, the geometric standard deviation (GSD) ranged from 1.9 to 2.2. The aerosol output and the amount of aerosol deposited in the IPL were determined gravimetrically by weighing the nebulizer (MicroSprayer) before and after each nebuliza- tion experiment. Pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of nebulized salbutamol formulations in the IPL A detailed description of the IPL as used for the pulmo- nary absorption and distribution experiments of salbutamol in this study can be found elsewhere.(18) Brieﬂy, for aerosol delivery of the three salbutamol formulations the Micro- Sprayer was integrated into the inspiratory tubing system with the tip of the MicroSprayer approximately 1 cm above the tracheal bifurcation. One milliliter of either the salbuta- mol solution (500 mg=mL) or the nanoparticle suspensions containing a total salbutamol amount of 500 mg=mL were nebulized into the IPL. The intratracheal aerosol application was synchronized with the inspiration phase and the dose was delivered when approximately 10 to 20% of the tidal volume had been inhaled (tidal volume 30 mL, frequency six strokes=min). After nebulization the ventilator frequency was reset to 30 strokes=min and the MicroSprayer was re- moved from the inspiratory tubing system. To determine the salbutamol perfusate concentrations, 800-mL samples were taken from the venous part of the system. The sampling in- terval was every 5 min for the ﬁrst hour, every 10 min for the second hour, and every 20 min thereafter up to a total of 240 min. Additionally, the salbutamol concentration in the collected perfusate droplets and in the bronchoalveolar la- vage ﬂuid was determined. Therefore, the lung was lavaged with a total of 150 mL of fresh perfusion ﬂuid at the end of the experiment. A 50-mL fraction was intratracheally in- stilled and reaspirated three times. The total recovery of la- vage ﬂuid was between 61 and 79%. Before measurement, all samples, the lavage and the dripping ﬂuid were centrifuged at 300g for 10 min to remove cells. The amount of salbu- tamol in the samples was determined by HPLC. To consider loss of perfusate during the experiments the measured salbutamol perfusate concentration was corrected by the following formula: ccorr(t) ¼ c(t)Vp(t)þ[Vp(0)Vp(t)] c(t) 2 Vp(0) ¼ c(t) 2  Vp(t) Vp(0) þ1   (1) where ccorr(t) is the corrected drug concentration in perfusate after time t, c(t) is the measured drug concentration in per- fusate after time t, Vp(t) is the perfusate volume after time t, and Vp(0) is the perfusate volume at the beginning. The drug absorption from the air space into the perfus- ate during the IPL experiments was then calculated as the mass fraction of the delivered dose recovered in the perfusate (% transferred of delivered dose; fractionperfusate). In addi- tion, the apparent ﬁrst-order fast (ka) and slow (kb) absorp- tion rate constants of salbutamol from the air space into the perfusate were calculated from the corresponding mass fraction of the delivered dose retained in the lung (% retained of delivered dose; fractionlung). The resulting curve can be described by the following biexponential equation: fractionlung(t) ¼ 100  fractionperfusate(t) ¼ Aekat þ Bekbt (2) where fractionlung(t) is the retained salbutamol fraction in the lung after time t, fractionperfusate(t) the measured salbutamol fraction in perfusate after time t, ka and kb are the apparent ﬁrst-order fast and slow absorption rate constants of salbu- 50 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

tamol from the air space into the perfusate, respectively, and A and B are the corresponding zero-time intercepts. Resolu- tion of the biexponential curve by the method of residuals enables the determination of all parameters in Equation (2).(19) HPLC assay for salbutamol Salbutamol concentrations were determined by HPLC using a Hewlett-Packard=Agilent 1100 series system (Agi- lent, Waldbronn, Germany) equipped with an autosampler (Smartline 3900, Knauer, Berlin, Germany). Samples (20 mL) were injected on a LiChroCART column (2504 mm, LiChrosphere 60, RP-select B, 5 mm) (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) at a temperature of 408C, with a mobile phase ﬂow rate of 1 mL=min. The mobile phase was composed of a 10=90 (v=v) mixture of methanol and a solution containing 36.6 mM sodium dihydrogenphosphate and 33.4 mM triethylamine, adjusted to pH 5 with phosphoric acid. All samples were assayed at a wavelength of 227 nm. The sal- butamol content was calculated using a calibration curve. Statistics All measurements were carried out in triplicate and values are presented as the mean  SD unless otherwise noted. Sta- tistical calculations were carried out using the software SigmaStat 3.5 (STATCON, Witzenhausen, Germany). To identify statistically signiﬁcant differences, one-way ANOVA with Bonferroni‘s post t-test analysis was performed. Prob- ability values of p < 0.05 were considered signiﬁcant and marked with an asterisk. Results Nanoparticle characteristics PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles were prepared at a concentration of 9.5 mg=mL polymer in water with a theo- retical drug loading of 5%. Salbutamol free base was used as drug in all experiments. The physicochemical properties of the salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles are given in Table 1. Representative AFM images of the two freshly prepared na- noparticle formulations are shown in Figure 2a and c. Freshly prepared salbutamol-loaded PLGA and VS(72)-10 nano- particles were of spherical shape. Both formulations showed similar mean particle sizes of approximately 120 nm (n ¼ 8) and narrow particle size distributions, displayed by PDI below 0.150 (n ¼ 8). The negative nanoparticle z-potential was more pronounced for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles (54.2  3.3 mV; mean  SD, n ¼ 8) compared to salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles (36.5  2.6 mV; mean  SD, n ¼ 8). The actual content of salbutamol was 488.6  17.3 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 8) for PLGA nanosus- pensions, containing a mixture of 365.4  26.4 mg=mL free and 123.1  24.3 mg=mL encapsulated salbutamol, and 498.4  32.1 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 8) for VS(72)-10 nanosuspen- sions, containing a mixture of 182.2  21.0 mg=mL free and 316.2  26.2 mg=mL encapsulated salbutamol. Thus, the preparation method assured an encapsulation efﬁciency of 25.2  4.9% (mean  SD, n ¼ 8) and 63.4  3.5% (mean  SD, n ¼ 8) for PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles, respectively, corresponding to actual drug loadings of 1.3  0.2% (w=w) (mean  SD, n ¼ 8) and 3.2  0.2% (w=w) (mean  SD, n ¼ 8). In vitro release studies The in vitro salbutamol release of both nanoparticle for- mulations before and after nebulization with the Micro- Sprayer was investigated in PBS pH 7.4 (Fig. 3). The actual salbutamol content of the freshly prepared formulations em- ployed in the in vitro experiments was 473.8  8.3 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) and 486.1  39.6 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) for PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanosuspensions, respectively. The actual salbutamol content of the nebulized formulations em- ployed in the in vitro experiments was 470.1  31.4 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) and 476.9  18.8 mg=mL (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) for PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanosuspensions, respectively. (a) Freshly prepared salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles, (b) nebulized salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles, (c) freshly pre- pared salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles, and (d) nebulized salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. nanoparticle formulations released the whole encapsulated amount of salbutamol (95 %) during incubation time. Freshly prepared salbutamol-loaded PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles released a total amount of 454.2  13.8 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) and 481.2  37.5 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 4), respectively. Nebulized salbutamol-loaded PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles released a total amount of 457.5  19.2 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) and 477.9  13.2 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 4), respectively. Only for nanoparticles prepared from the VS(72-10) polymer a sustained release of salbutamol was observed over 1 to 2 h. Nanoparticle drug release behavior after nebulization did negligibly differ from that of freshly prepared nanoparticles. No loss of salbutamol was detected during incubation time. Nanoparticle stability during nebulization The results from the investigation of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles after nebulization utilizing the MicroSprayer are shown in Table 2. Representative AFM images of the two nebulized nanoparticle formulations are depicted in Figure 2b and 2d. Nebulized salbutamol-loaded PLGA nano- particles displayed a signiﬁcant increase in mean particle size by approximately 10 nm and in polydispersity index during the process of nebulization ( p < 0.05), whereas z-potential and encapsulation efﬁciency were not signiﬁcantly affected (n ¼ 8). For salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles no signiﬁcant change in particle size, z-potential and encapsu- lation efﬁciency was detected (n ¼ 8), only the slightly in- creased polydispersity index after nebulization was found to be statistically signiﬁcant ( p < 0.05). MicroSprayer characterization The total aerosol output, determined gravimetrically by weighing the nebulizer before and after exhaustive nebuli- zation of the reservoir, and deposited salbutamol mass in the IPL experiments amounted to 0.95  0.02 g (mean  SD, n ¼ 6) and 490.3  31.1 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 6) for salbutamol solution, 0.96  0.02 g (mean  SD, n ¼ 5) and 509.7  17.2 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 5), containing a mixture of 388.1  36.1 mg free and 121.6  29.2 mg encapsulated salbutamol for PLGA nanoparticles, and 0.96  0.03 g (mean  SD, n ¼ 4) and 511.0  20.2 mg (mean  SD, n ¼ 6), containing a mixture of 191.5  4.6 mg free and 319.5  21.5 mg encapsulated salbuta- mol for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. No statistically signiﬁcant differences were found between the deposition values for salbutamol solution and both salbutamol-loaded nano- particle formulations. Pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of nebulized salbutamol formulations in the IPL The absorption proﬁles of salbutamol into the perfus- ate following pulmonary administration are depicted in Figure 4. The salbutamol absorption from the air space into the perfusate during the IPL experiments was characterized by two distinct phases for all formulations. The initial con- tinuous increase of the transferred salbutamol amount into the perfusate lasted for approximately 30 min and was fol- lowed by a slower second absorption phase. Within the ini- tial absorption phase all curves superimposed, whereas the second absorption phase disclosed differences between the three formulations. The absorption from salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles was slower compared to salbutamol solution and salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles. No plateau was reached throughout the IPL experiments. Table 3 summarizes the pharmacokinetic parameters for the salbutamol formulation exposures to the IPL. All formulations showed similar apparent ﬁrst-order fast ab- sorption rate constants, but distinct results for the three formulations were obtained for the ﬁrst-order slow absorp- tion rate constant. Although only negligible differences were seen for salbutamol solution and salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles, signiﬁcant differences were observed for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles ( p < 0.05) with a reduced amount of transferred salbutamol for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles ( p < 0.05). The ﬁnal salbutamol distribution within the experimental system is given in Figure 5. After administration of aero- FIG. 3. In vitro drug release proﬁles of PLGA (circles) and VS(72)-10 (squares) nanoparticles loaded with salbutamol in PBS pH 7.4. Discussion Nanoparticle characteristics Numerous manufacturing techniques are known for the production of drug-loaded polymeric nanoparticles, allowing extensive modulation of their characteristics. Conventionally, two groups of preparation methods can be distinguished. The ﬁrst involves polymerization of monomers, whereas the sec- ond is based on precipitation of preformed synthetic poly- mers. The solvent displacement technique that has been used for the production of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles in this study is a typical example for the second group.(20) This technique involves the use of an organic solvent that is com- pletely miscible with water, typically acetone. Progressive addition of polymer dissolved in acetone to an aqueous phase under stirring leads to the formation of colloidal particles. The mechanism of particle formation is explained by interfacial turbulences between the two liquid phases which are gov- erned by the Marangoni effect.(16) Accordingly, the originally elliptic-shaped structure of the injected solvent layer breaks down into small droplets, which again break down into smaller and smaller parts until non divisible polymer aggre- gates are left, thereby allowing reproducible production of polymeric nanoparticles in a size range from 100 to 200 nm with narrow size distributions (Table 1 and Fig. 2). The usefulness of the solvent displacement technique is often limited to lipophilic drugs that are highly soluble in polar organic solvents, but only slightly soluble in water, to avoid extensive loss of the drug into the aqueous phase FIG. 4. during nanoparticle formation. Encapsulation of water- soluble drugs is a difﬁcult task, due to the low afﬁnity of the drug compounds to the polymers on the one hand, and the short distances drugs have to cover to diffuse out of the particles on the other hand.(21) Thus, poor incorporation of the low molecular weight hydrophilic drug salbutamol into nanoparticles prepared from the hydrophobic polymer PLGA was observed (Table 1). The gained encapsulation efﬁciency was in good agreement with previous reports.(22,23) The solvent selection and addition of different excipients to the inner organic phase, like surfactants, was found to im- prove encapsulation efﬁciency. The same results were made by manipulating the outer water phase by pH changes or addition of salts.(24,25) However, these methods were not considered for the preparation of salbutamol-loaded nano- particles by the solvent displacement technique used in this study. Our approach included the synthesis of a novel class of biodegradable branched polyesters suitable for nano- particle production.(11) The introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure would promote electrostatic interactions with oppositely charged drugs, thereby improving the design of nanoparti- culate carriers as drug delivery systems to the lung. Surface characterization studies of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles suggested that both nanoparticle formulations exhibited a negative surface charge, displayed by a negative z-potential (Table 1). An increased negative surface charge was detected for salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles prepared from VS(72)-10 due to the introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure. It has been previ- ously reported that small molecules can be complexed and encapsulated efﬁciently via electrostatic interactions with polyelectrolytes.(26) This concept is also proposed for the salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. Vigorously mix- ing of salbutamol together with the VS(72)-10 polymer prior to the nanoparticle preparation process ensued a high sal- butamol encapsulation efﬁciency (Table 1). In vitro release studies Drug release from pulmonary drug delivery systems often depends on the used delivery system as well as on the physicochemical properties of the drug. Therefore, the in vitro release proﬁles of the salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle prep- arations were investigated in PBS pH 7.4. Freshly prepared salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles showed no retarda- tion of drug release, whereas for freshly prepared salbuta- mol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles a sustained release over 1 to 2 h was observed. Neither a difference in curve pro- gression nor a difference in the released amount of salbuta- mol was seen after nebulization for both formulations. Both nanoparticle formulations released the whole encapsulated amount of salbutamol during incubation time (Fig. 3). The release rates of drug compounds from nanoparticulate drug delivery systems is thought to be dependent upon desorp- tion of the surfacebound, adsorbed drug, diffusion through the nanoparticle matrix, rate of polymer degradation, and a combined process of degradation and diffusion. Thus, dif- fusion and biodegradation govern the process of drug re- lease.(27) We suppose that salbutamol release from PLGA nanoparticles was attributed to the low afﬁnity of the drug to the polymer,(22,23) as also indicated by the low encapsulation efﬁciency. Dilution of the nanoparticle suspensions with an excess of PBS at pH 7.4 favored diffusion of the encap- sulated drug into the release medium. In a previous study, Lai et al.(2) have prepared PLGA microparticles loaded with isoproterenol (mean diameter: 4.5 mm) for sustained bron- chodilation experiments in rats. Upon dilution with instilla- tion medium a large burst release was found, despite a longer distance the drug had to cover to diffuse out of the microparticles. The particles released about 70% of the in- corporated drug within 5 min, displaying the low afﬁnity of the base isoproterenol to the PLGA matrix. An introduction of negatively charged functional groups in the structure of the VS(72)-10 polymer promoted electrostatic interactions between the polymeric nanoparticles and the oppositely charged salbutamol, resulting in higher encapsulation efﬁ- ciency and sustained release of salbutamol. Nanoparticle stability during nebulization Among the large number of synthetic polymers available for the preparation of polymeric nanoparticles, the most commonly used belong to the family of polyesters. These polymers are known to exhibit excellent biocompatibility and adequate biodegradation under physiological conditions. However, linear polyesters like PLGA have many limitations as nanoparticulate drug delivery systems to the lungs. First, PLGA nanoparticles degrade over a period of weeks to months, but typically deliver drugs for a shorter period of time.(8) Such a pattern would lead to an unwanted accumu- lation of polymer in the lungs when repeated administrations are needed. Second, PLGA nanoparticles have hydrophobic surfaces, which result in particle aggregation upon nebuliza- tion.(28) Branched polyesters like VS(72)-10 show signiﬁcant potential to overcome the drawbacks of linear polyesters. Due to their unique properties, such as biocompatibility and con- trolled variability of biodegradation rates, they are highly suited for pulmonary application. Through polymer modiﬁ- cation biodegradation rates may be manipulated to achieve half-lives ranging from several hours to several weeks.(9,11) Further, their amphiphilic character and the resulting in- crease in hydrophilicity provide advantages when nebuliza- tion is required. In this study the MicroSprayer was used for nebulization of the nanoparticle suspensions. The investiga- tion of nanoparticle formulation stability to the aerosolization process ensued that salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles were prone to aggregation during the process of nebulization, displayed by a signiﬁcant increase in particle size and PDI ( p < 0.05), whereas z-potential and encapsulation efﬁciency were not signiﬁcantly affected by nebulization. For salbuta- mol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles only a negligible change in particle size, z-potential, and encapsulation efﬁciency was detected. Although statistically signiﬁcant, the difference in the polydispersity index before and after nebulization was smaller when compared with the difference for salbutamol- loaded PLGA nanoparticles (Table 2). The size measurements gained by PCS were in good agreement with the AFM data (Fig. 2). MicroSprayer characterization In this study the MicroSprayer was used for intratracheal aerosol application to the IPL. The aerosol delivery was syn- chronized with the inspiration phase and the dose was 54 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

delivered when approximately 10 to 20% of the tidal volume had been inhaled. The aerosol deposition in isolated, per- fused, and ventilated lung models has been demonstrated to be inversely proportional to the respiratory frequency.(29) Accordingly, the breathing frequency in the IPL experiments was decreased from 30 to 6 strokes=min during nebulization, resulting in a longer inhalation period with lower ﬂow rate, to facilitate a peripheral lung deposition. Tronde et al.(30) have used a similar method, an intratracheal nebulization catheter technique synchronized with inspiration, for the delivery of aerosols to small rodents and isolated rat lungs. In the isolated and perfused lung, they achieved high total aerosol deposi- tion (*100%) and demonstrated that about 40% of the de- posited aerosol dose was distributed to the parenchymal region. Beck et al.(31) investigated the regional deposition of aerosols delivered to the lungs of monkeys by means of a MicroSprayer. Analysis of their data demonstrates a pe- ripheral lung deposition of approximately 40%. Because the aerodynamic properties, displayed by the MMAD and GSD, are important parameters for aerosol deposition within the lungs, the size distribution of the formulation aerosols was determined by laser diffraction. This method is well estab- lished for size analysis of aerosols, and has shown good cor- relation with pulmonary deposition ﬁndings.(32) The MMAD and GSD of aerosolized saline and all salbutamol formula- tions in this study were in the range of previously reported magnitudes for other liquids.(14,31) Therefore, a total aerosol deposition of 100% of the nebulized salbutamol formulations with a signiﬁcant delivery to peripheral lung regions can be expected when intratracheally nebulized and synchronized with inspiration in the isolated rabbit lung. Pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of nebulized salbutamol formulations in the IPL The promising in vitro results led us to the examination of both salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle formulations as po- tential pulmonary drug delivery systems in separate IPL experiments. Therefore, the pulmonary absorption and dis- tribution characteristics of salbutamol after aerosolization as solution or entrapped into nanoparticles were veriﬁed. As expected from previous investigations demonstrating good biocompatibility of intrapulmonary administered polymeric nanoparticles,(33) the isolated lungs fulﬁlled the three claimed criteria after nebulization of the salbutamol-loaded nano- particles. During the whole experiment they maintained homogeneous white appearance with no signs of hemostasis, edema, or atelectasis; a pulmonary artery and ventilation pressure in the normal range; and isogravimetry. The sal- butamol absorption from the air space into the perfusate during the IPL experiments was characterized by two dis- tinct phases for all formulations (Table 2 and Fig. 4). The initial continuous increase of the transferred salbutamol into the perfusate was followed by a slower second absorption phase. A similar pulmonary absorption proﬁle has been reported previously for the hydrophilic ﬂuorescence dye 6- carboxyﬂuorescein in rats.(34) It was proposed that the 6- carboxyﬂuorescein absorption proﬁle from the lung after intratracheal instillation was incongruous with a simple ﬁrst- order input. Therefore, they suggested an absorption model involving two simultaneous ﬁrst-order absorption processes by both active transport and passage through the pores of the epithelium membrane. The drug absorption proﬁle dur- ing the initial phase from salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles hardly differed from those of salbutamol solution, as indi- cated by similar apparent ﬁrst-order fast absorption rate constants ka (Table 3). Hence, during the initial phase of drug absorption by the IPL the unencapsulated salbutamol from both nanoparticle formulations (75 and 40% for PLGA and VS(72)-10, respectively; Tables 1 and 2) appeared in the perfusate. The following second absorption phase disclosed differences between the three formulations. Only negligible differences were seen for salbutamol solution and salbutamol- loaded PLGA nanoparticles, as expected by the low encap- sulation efﬁciency and fast drug release in the in vitro studies. Signiﬁcant slower drug absorption from salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles compared to salbutamol solution was observed and added up to a retention of salbutamol in the lung tissue, thereby providing a sustained pharmaco- logical effect (Table 3 and Fig. 4). The slower drug absorption phase for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles corre- sponds to the sustained drug release in vitro. Regarding the lung architecture, there are only small amounts of liquid at the surface of the respiratory tract. Thus, the reduced dilu- tion effect in the lung after aerosolization of the VS(72)-10 nanoparticle suspensions results in an even more pro- nounced retard effect compared to the in vitro situation. However, it should be appreciated that the biexponential method used for analysis of the IPL perfusate data can produce misleading results. The values for the slow ab- sorption rate constant, kb, in particular are very susceptible to errors due to (1) small changes in the amounts retained within the formulation in vivo and (2) changes in regional deposition that may occur between formulations. The IPL preparation as used studies absorption into the pulmonary circulation; the bronchial circulation is severed, and drug substance depositing in the upper airways may be unavail- able for absorption into the perfusate, irrespective of its physical form. The gained absorption proﬁle and signiﬁcantly decreased total recovery for the salbutamol from VS(72)-10 nano- particles (49%) compared to the total recovery for salbutamol from solution (75%) (Fig. 5), might have been attributed to internalization of salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles into lung cells. In fact, Dailey et al.(8) demonstrated that PLGA-derived nanoparticles were taken up by A549 cells. Assuming the localization of VS(72)-10 nanoparticles in the interstitial spaces, dilution of the nanoparticle suspension would have led to a release of encapsulated salbutamol with consequent rapid absorption into the perfusate. Additionally, an increased amount of salbutamol would have been found in the lavage ﬂuid. But this was not the case. Thus, studies on the localization of the nanoparticles in lung cells and in dif- ferent lung compartments after aerosol application are indi- cated to fully explain the absorption and distribution patterns reported in this study. Conclusion In conclusion, the results of this study highlight the po- tential of colloidal pulmonary drug delivery systems for the treatment of respiratory diseases. Polymeric nanoparticles were prepared from two different biodegradable polyes- ters by a solvent displacement method. High encapsulation PULMONARY TARGETING WITH SALBUTAMOL-LOADED NANOPARTICLES 55

efﬁciencies for salbutamol were only achieved using the bio- compatible, branched polyester VS(72)-10. In vitro release studies showed a sustained release of the encapsulated sal- butamol from the VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. Investigation of nanoparticle formulation stability to the aerosolization process utilizing the MicroSprayer ensured that the characteristics and encapsulation efﬁciencies were only negligibly affected by nebulization for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. After inhalative administration of salbutamol formulations to the IPL, salbu- tamol showed a reproducible pulmonary absorption pro- ﬁle characterized by two distinct phases for all formulations. The initial drug absorption phase exhibited superimposed curves, whereas the second absorption phase disclosed slower absorption behavior for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles compared to salbutamol solution. Thus, the absorption degree of salbutamol released from VS(72)-10 na- noparticles by the IPL was less than those of salbutamol from solution denoted by a lower total recovery. The in vitro release proﬁle was in agreement with the ex vivo absorption proﬁle. The amount of salbutamol that remained in the other com- partments of the IPL was similar for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles and solution indicating an uptake of the salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles into pulmonary cells. In summary, biocompatible salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles demonstrated a pulmonary sustained drug release after in- halative administration. The results from this ex vivo study will hopefully promote progress in nanomedicine. Pulmonary Targeting with Biodegradable Salbutamol-Loaded Nanoparticles Moritz Beck-Broichsitter,1,2 Julia Gauss,1 Tobias Gessler, Ph.D.,1 Werner Seeger, M.D.,1 Thomas Kissel, Ph.D.,2 and Thomas Schmehl, Ph.D.1 Abstract Background: Aerosol therapy using particulate drug carriers has become an increasingly attractive method to deliver therapeutic or diagnostic compounds to the lung. Polymeric nanoparticles are widely investigated car- riers in nanomedicine. The targeted and controlled release of drugs from nanoparticles for pulmonary delivery, however, is a research ﬁeld that has been so far rather unexploited. Therefore, the objective of this study was to compare the pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of salbutamol after aerosolization as solution or entrapped into novel polymeric nanoparticles in an isolated rabbit lung model (IPL). Methods: Physicochemical properties, morphology, encapsulation efﬁciency, in vitro drug release, stability of nanoparticles to nebulization, as well as pulmonary drug absorption and distribution after nebulization in the IPL were investigated. Results: Salbutamol-loaded poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA) and poly(vinyl sulfonate-co-vinyl alcohol)- graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (VS(72)-10) nanoparticles were prepared by a modiﬁed solvent displace- ment technique with a mean particle size of approximately 120 nm and a polydispersity index below 0.150. VS(72)-10 nanoparticles showed a more negative z-potential of 54.2  3.3 mV compared to PLGA nanoparticles (36.5  2.6 mV). Salbutamol encapsulation efﬁciency was 25.2  4.9% and 63.4  3.5% for PLGA and VS(72)-10 nanoparticles, respectively. After nebulization utilizing the MicroSprayer physicochemical properties of salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles were virtually unchanged, whereas nebulized salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles showed a signiﬁcant increase in mean particle size and polydispersity. In vitro release studies demonstrated a sustained release of the encapsulated salbutamol from VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. In parallel, a sustained salbutamol release proﬁle was observed after aerosol delivery of these particles to the IPL as reﬂected by a lower salbutamol recovery in the perfusate (40.2  5.8%) when compared to PLGA nanoparticles (55.2  9.1%) and salbutamol solution (62.8  7.1%). Conclusions: The current study suggests that inhalative delivery of biodegradable nanoparticles may be a viable approach for the treatment of respiratory diseases. Key words: nanoparticles; salbutamol; biodegradable branched polyesters; nebulization; isolated lung; pulmo- nary drug delivery; targeting; sustained release Introduction P ulmonary drug delivery has become a well-established approach in the treatment of local respiratory diseases, and provides an excellent example of targeted drug therapy. Targeted drug delivery to the lungs is attractive for several reasons. With the direct delivery of therapeutic agents to the desired site of action, rapid onset of drug action, lower sys- temic exposure, and consequently, reduced side effects can be achieved. A signiﬁcant disadvantage of inhalation therapy is the relatively short duration of the pharmacological effects demanding multiple daily inhalation maneuvers.(1) Strategies for further improvement of inhalation ther- apy include development of pulmonary controlled release 1Medical Clinic II, Department of Internal Medicine, Justus-Liebig-University Giessen, Klinikstrasse 36, D-35392 Giessen, Germany. 2Department of Pharmaceutics and Biopharmacy, Philipps-University, Marburg, Ketzerbach 63, D-35037 Marburg, Germany. JOURNAL OF AEROSOL MEDICINE AND PULMONARY DRUG DELIVERY Volume 23, Number 1, 2010 ª Mary Ann Liebert, Inc. Pp. 47–57 DOI: 10.1089=jamp.2009.0759 47

formulations, for example, microparticles.(2) In recent years nanomedicine has become an attractive concept for the tar- geted delivery of therapeutic(3) and diagnostic(4) compounds to the lung. Among the various drug delivery systems con- sidered for pulmonary application the use of biodegradable polymeric nanoparticles represents several advantages for the treatment of respiratory diseases. A number of different strategies have been proposed for modiﬁcation of nano- particle characteristics to control their behavior within bio- logical environments, like cell-speciﬁc targeted drug delivery or modiﬁed biological distribution of drugs, both at the cel- lular and organ level.(5,6) They hold great promise to over- come the stringent requirements placed on these delivery systems, such as ability to be transferred into an aerosol, protection of drugs against degradation, biocompatibility, targeting of speciﬁc sites or cell populations in the lung, and release of the drug in a predetermined manner.(7) In addition to biocompatibility, the degradability of the polymeric drug carriers within an acceptable period of time must also be taken into account. Slowly or nondegrading polymers may lead to accumulation in the lung when repeated application is nec- essary. For the above-mentioned reasons, most common biodegradable polymers with prolonged biodegradation rates such as poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA), are not suitable for application in the respiratory tract. One way to overcome this problem is to synthesize polymers with faster degradation. Fast degrading polymers, composed of short PLGA chains grafted onto charge-modiﬁed poly(vinyl alco- hol) (PVA) backbones, have been designed for drug delivery. The adjustable properties of these branched polyesters make them highly suitable for pulmonary formulations.(8–10) Wang et al.(11) have recently synthesized a new class of negatively charged branched polyesters, namely, poly(vinyl sulfonate- co-vinyl alcohol)-graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide), abbre- viated as VS, suitable for nanoparticle production. The introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure would promote electrostatic interac- tions with oppositely charged drugs, like salbutamol, thereby improving the design of nanoparticulate carriers as drug de- livery systems to the lung. Different methods are used to assess the behavior of drug- loaded pulmonary delivery systems ranging from in vitro to in vivo systems.(12) Ex vivo isolated, perfused, and ventilated lungs are commonly used models to investigate the phar- macokinetics of inhaled drugs.(13–15) In ex vivo lung models, lung-speciﬁc pharmacokinetic effects, like drug absorption and distribution proﬁles, can be investigated without the contribution of systemic absorption, distribution, and elimi- nation of the drug. Currently a large number of approved therapeutic agents are on the market for treatment of respiratory diseases. However, no sustained release formulation for pulmonary drug delivery has been marketed so far, despite the in- creasing interest and progress in pharmaceutical research. The aim of the current study was to compare the pulmo- nary absorption and distribution characteristics of the hy- drophilic drug salbutamol, a commonly used short-acting b2-agonist for symptom management of asthma, after aero- solization as either solution or entrapped into two different nanoparticle formulations in an isolated, perfused, and ventilated rabbit lung model (IPL). The preparation method of nanoparticles made of a linear polyester (PLGA) and a promising new class of biocompatible, branched polyesters (VS), respectively, loaded with salbutamol by a modiﬁed solvent displacement technique is described. The particle size, polydispersity index, z-potential, particle morphology, drug encapsulation efﬁciency, and in vitro drug release of the prepared salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle formulations were investigated using photon correlation spectroscopy (PCS), laser Doppler anemometry (LDA), atomic force microscopy (AFM), and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). Furthermore, the stability of the nanoparticles to the nebulization procedure utilizing a MicroSprayer was de- termined using the above-mentioned techniques. The aerosol characteristics of all formulations were analyzed by laser light scattering. Finally, the pulmonary drug absorption proﬁles for the three formulations after nebulization in an IPL were compared by monitoring the salbutamol concen- tration in the perfusate with time. Additionally, drug dis- tribution in the different compartments of the IPL was evaluated at the end of the experiments. Materials and Methods Materials The biocompatible, linear polyester, namely, poly(D,L- lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA), Resomer RG 502 H was ac- quired from Boehringer Ingelheim (Ingelheim, Germany). The biocompatible, branched polyester, namely, poly(vinyl sulfonate-co-vinyl alcohol)-graft-poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide), was synthesized and characterized as described previ- ously.(11) These branched polyesters were speciﬁcally de- signed for drug delivery to the lung and are comprised of short PLGA chains grafted onto a sulfonate substituted PVA backbone. As abbreviation A(x)-y is used where A decribes the type of backbone substitution (VS ¼ vinyl sulfonate), x the total average number of sulfonate functions on the PVA backbone, and y the PLGA side-chain length. In this study VS(72)-10 was employed. The molecular structures of both polymers used as nanoparticle matrix material are depicted in Figure 1. The b2-agonist salbutamol (free base) was a gift from Boehringer Ingelheim. All other chemicals and solvents used in this study were of the highest analytical grade com- mercially available. Methods Preparation of salbutamol solution. The drug solution containing salbutamol was prepared in isotonic phosphate- buffered saline (PBS) at pH 7.4 to yield a ﬁnal concentration of 500 mg=mL. Preparation of nanoparticles. Salbutamol-loaded nano- particles were prepared by a modiﬁed solvent displacement technique.(16) To avoid artiﬁcial surfactants, the preparation was modiﬁed in the following manner: polymer (47.5 mg) and drug (2.5 mg) were dissolved together in 1 mL of acetone at 258C. The resulting solution was injected into a magneti- cally stirred (500 rpm) aqueous phase of 5 mL of ﬁltrated and double-distilled water (pH 7.0, conductance 0.055 mS=cm, 258C) using a special apparatus. The apparatus consists of an electronically adjustable single-suction pump, which was used to inject the organic solution into the aqueous phase through an injection needle (Fine-Ject 0.630 mm) at a 48 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

constant ﬂow rate (10.0 mL=min). The pump rate was regu- lated and constantly monitored by electric power control. After injection of the organic phase, the resulting colloidal suspension was stirred for *3 h under reduced pressure to remove the organic solvent. Particles were characterized and used directly after preparation. Physicochemical characterization of nanoparticles Particle size measurement. The particle size distribution of the obtained nanoparticles was determined by PCS using a Zetasizer NanoZS=ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments, Herren- berg, Germany). The analysis was performed at a tem- perature of 258C using samples appropriately diluted with ﬁltrated and double-distilled water to avoid multiscattering events. The DTS V. 5.10-software was used to calculate particle mean diameter (Z-Ave) and the width of the ﬁtted Gaussian distribution, which is displayed as the polydis- persity index (PDI). Each size measurement was performed with at least 10 runs. All measurements were carried out in triplicate directly after nanoparticle preparation. z-potential measurement. The z-potential was measured by LDA using a Zetasizer NanoZS=ZEN3600 (Malvern In- struments). The analysis was performed at a temperature of 258C using samples appropriately diluted with 1.56 mM NaCl solution to maintain a constant ionic strength. The DTS V. 5.10-software was used to calculate the average z-potential values from the data of multiple runs. All measurements were carried out in triplicate directly after nanoparticle preparation. AFM Morphology of nanoparticles was analyzed by AFM. Samples were prepared by placing a sample volume of 10 mL onto commercial glass slides. The slides were incubated with the nanoparticle suspension for 10 min before washing twice with distilled water and drying in a stream of dry nitrogen. Samples were investigated within 2 h after preparation. AFM was performed using a NanoWizard (JPK Instruments, Berlin, Germany) in intermittent contact mode to avoid damage to the sample surface. Commercially available Si3N4 tips attached to I-type cantilevers with a length of 230 mm and a nominal force constant of 40 N=m (NSC16 AlBS, Micromasch, Tallinn, Estonia) were used. The scan fre- quency was between 0.5 and 1 Hz and antiproportional to the scan size. The results were visualized as trace signal in amplitude mode. Determination of entrapped salbutamol To determine encapsulation efﬁciency samples of nano- particle suspensions (175 mL) were subjected to ultracentri- fugation (Airfuge, Beckman Coulter, Krefeld, Germany) at 110,000 rpm (199,000g) for 15 min at 48C. After centrifuga- tion the supernatant was carefully removed and sampled to determine the amount of unencapsulated salbutamol. The remaining pellets were freeze dried (Beta II, Christ, Osterode, Germany), weighed, and then dissolved in chloroform. After complete dissolution acidic water (adjusted to pH 2.5 with phosphoric acid) was added to the chloroform phase to ex- tract salbutamol. The two phases were mixed vigorously for 1 h and then centrifuged for 5 min at 16,000g (Centrifuge 5418, Eppendorf, Hamburg, Germany) to achieve phase sep- aration. A sample was taken from the aqueous phase to determine the amount of encapsulated salbutamol. Salbuta- mol concentrations were determined by HPLC as described below. In vitro release studies The in vitro drug release studies were carried out in PBS pH 7.4 over a 240-min period of time, according to the run time of the experiments with the IPL model. The studies were performed with nanoparticle suspensions with a theo- retical salbutamol content of 500 mg=mL. Samples of the na- noparticle suspensions (1 mL) were transferred to plastic tubes and diluted with the release medium resulting in a volume of 3 mL. Incubation occurred at 378C while under- going vertical rotation (20 rpm) (Rotatherm, Gebr. Liebisch, Bielefeld, Germany). Samples of 175 mL were taken at pre- determined time points and centrifuged as described above prior to determining the cumulative release of salbutamol by HPLC. In parallel, salbutamol was incubated in the release medium under the same conditions to exclude loss of sal- butamol during incubation time. IPL model The IPL model has been previously described.(17) Brieﬂy, male rabbits weighing 2.5 to 3.5 kg were gradually anesthe- tized with a mixture of ketamine (Ketavet, Pharmacia, Erlangen, Germany) and xylazine (Rompun, Bayer Vital, Leverkusen, Germany) in a ratio of 3 to 2 and anticoagulated with heparin (1000 U=kg). After achieving a deep anestheti- zation the animals were ventilated with room air via a tracheal cannula using a Harvard respirator (cat=rabbit Ventilator, Hugo Sachs Elektronik, March Hugstetten, Germany). Catheters were inserted into the left atrium and the pul- monary artery after a midsternal thoracotomy. Perfusion with ice-cold Krebs-Henseleit buffer (Serag-Wiessner, Naila= Bayern, Germany) with a pulsatile ﬂow of 10–20 mL=min was immediately started. The lungs were removed from the thorax and placed in a temperature equilibrated chamber at 48C, freely suspended from a force transducer for monitoring the lung weight. Then the perfusion ﬂow was slowly increased to 100 mL=min. At the same time the temperature of the perfu- sion ﬂuid and the housing chamber was raised to 398C. After a 30-min steady-state period, the perfusion ﬂuid was exchanged once by fresh buffer (total volume 345 mL). With the onset of artiﬁcial perfusion an air mixture of 21% O2, 5.3% CO2, and 73.7% N2 was used for ventilation (tidal volume 30 mL, fre- quency 30 strokes=min). A positive end-expiratory pressure of 1 cm H2O was used throughout. Pulmonary arterial pressure (PAP), pulmonary venous pressure (PVP, measured in the left atrium), inﬂation pressure, and the weight of the isolated lung were registered continuously. Left atrial pressure was set 1.5 mmHg in all experiments. Only those lungs were selected for the study that fulﬁlled three criteria: ﬁrst, homogeneous white appearance with no signs of hemostasis, edema, or at- electasis; second, a pulmonary artery and ventilation pres- sure in the normal range; and third, no weight gain during the steady-state period. Sampling, evaporation and ﬂuid dripping caused losses of perfusate during the experiments (in total *55 mL=240 min). The perfusate drops that escaped from the surface of the lungs (*4 mL=240 min) were collected and analyzed. Nanoparticle stability during nebulization The stability of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles to neb- ulization with the MicroSprayer (Model IA-1B, Penn- Century, Philadelphia, PA) was investigated prior to the IPL experiments. The device is based on a stainless steel tube (19-gauge) and is operated by a plastic syringe. The aerosol is generated by an atomizer at the very tip of the tube (diameter: 1.5 mm), thus permitting to deliver aerosols directly into the lung. Brieﬂy, samples of 1-mL suspension were aerosolized to exhaustive nebulization of the reservoir. The nebulized aerosol was collected in a 15 mL tube for further analysis. The stability of the nebulized nanoparticle suspensions was investigated using PCS, LDA, and AFM. In addition, the amount of encapsulated salbutamol of the aerosolized nanoparticles was measured as described above. MicroSprayer characterization Aerosol particle sizes of NaCl 0.9% (m=v), salbutamol solution, salbutamol-loaded PLGA, and VS(72)-10 nano- particle suspensions were determined using laser light scat- tering (HELOS, Sympatec, Clausthal-Zellerfeld, Germany). All data were analyzed in the Mie mode. The mass median aerodynamic diameters (MMAD) of the aerosol droplets generated from the MicroSprayer were in the range of 27 to 32 mm for all nebulized formulations, the geometric standard deviation (GSD) ranged from 1.9 to 2.2. The aerosol output and the amount of aerosol deposited in the IPL were determined gravimetrically by weighing the nebulizer (MicroSprayer) before and after each nebuliza- tion experiment. Pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of nebulized salbutamol formulations in the IPL A detailed description of the IPL as used for the pulmo- nary absorption and distribution experiments of salbutamol in this study can be found elsewhere.(18) Brieﬂy, for aerosol delivery of the three salbutamol formulations the Micro- Sprayer was integrated into the inspiratory tubing system with the tip of the MicroSprayer approximately 1 cm above the tracheal bifurcation. One milliliter of either the salbuta- mol solution (500 mg=mL) or the nanoparticle suspensions containing a total salbutamol amount of 500 mg=mL were nebulized into the IPL. The intratracheal aerosol application was synchronized with the inspiration phase and the dose was delivered when approximately 10 to 20% of the tidal volume had been inhaled (tidal volume 30 mL, frequency six strokes=min). After nebulization the ventilator frequency was reset to 30 strokes=min and the MicroSprayer was re- moved from the inspiratory tubing system. To determine the salbutamol perfusate concentrations, 800-mL samples were taken from the venous part of the system. The sampling in- terval was every 5 min for the ﬁrst hour, every 10 min for the second hour, and every 20 min thereafter up to a total of 240 min. Additionally, the salbutamol concentration in the collected perfusate droplets and in the bronchoalveolar la- vage ﬂuid was determined. Therefore, the lung was lavaged with a total of 150 mL of fresh perfusion ﬂuid at the end of the experiment. A 50-mL fraction was intratracheally in- stilled and reaspirated three times. The total recovery of la- vage ﬂuid was between 61 and 79%. Before measurement, all samples, the lavage and the dripping ﬂuid were centrifuged at 300g for 10 min to remove cells. The amount of salbu- tamol in the samples was determined by HPLC. To consider loss of perfusate during the experiments the measured salbutamol perfusate concentration was corrected by the following formula: ccorr(t) ¼ c(t)Vp(t)þ[Vp(0)Vp(t)] c(t) 2 Vp(0) ¼ c(t) 2  Vp(t) Vp(0) þ1   (1) where ccorr(t) is the corrected drug concentration in perfusate after time t, c(t) is the measured drug concentration in per- fusate after time t, Vp(t) is the perfusate volume after time t, and Vp(0) is the perfusate volume at the beginning. The drug absorption from the air space into the perfus- ate during the IPL experiments was then calculated as the mass fraction of the delivered dose recovered in the perfusate (% transferred of delivered dose; fractionperfusate). In addi- tion, the apparent ﬁrst-order fast (ka) and slow (kb) absorp- tion rate constants of salbutamol from the air space into the perfusate were calculated from the corresponding mass fraction of the delivered dose retained in the lung (% retained of delivered dose; fractionlung). The resulting curve can be described by the following biexponential equation: fractionlung(t) ¼ 100  fractionperfusate(t) ¼ Aekat þ Bekbt (2) where fractionlung(t) is the retained salbutamol fraction in the lung after time t, fractionperfusate(t) the measured salbutamol fraction in perfusate after time t, ka and kb are the apparent ﬁrst-order fast and slow absorption rate constants of salbu- 50 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

tamol from the air space into the perfusate, respectively, and A and B are the corresponding zero-time intercepts. Resolu- tion of the biexponential curve by the method of residuals enables the determination of all parameters in Equation (2).(19) HPLC assay for salbutamol Salbutamol concentrations were determined by HPLC using a Hewlett-Packard=Agilent 1100 series system (Agi- lent, Waldbronn, Germany) equipped with an autosampler (Smartline 3900, Knauer, Berlin, Germany). Samples (20 mL) were injected on a LiChroCART column (2504 mm, LiChrosphere 60, RP-select B, 5 mm) (Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) at a temperature of 408C, with a mobile phase ﬂow rate of 1 mL=min. The mobile phase was composed of a 10=90 (v=v) mixture of methanol and a solution containing 36.6 mM sodium dihydrogenphosphate and 33.4 mM triethylamine, adjusted to pH 5 with phosphoric acid. All samples were assayed at a wavelength of 227 nm. The sal- butamol content was calculated using a calibration curve. Statistics All measurements were carried out in triplicate and values are presented as the mean  SD unless otherwise noted. Sta- tistical calculations were carried out using the software SigmaStat 3.5 (STATCON, Witzenhausen, Germany). To identify statistically signiﬁcant differences, one-way ANOVA with Bonferroni‘s post t-test analysis was performed. Prob- ability values of p < 0.05 were considered signiﬁcant and marked with an asterisk. Discussion Nanoparticle characteristics Numerous manufacturing techniques are known for the production of drug-loaded polymeric nanoparticles, allowing extensive modulation of their characteristics. Conventionally, two groups of preparation methods can be distinguished. The ﬁrst involves polymerization of monomers, whereas the sec- ond is based on precipitation of preformed synthetic poly- mers. The solvent displacement technique that has been used for the production of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles in this study is a typical example for the second group.(20) This technique involves the use of an organic solvent that is com- pletely miscible with water, typically acetone. Progressive addition of polymer dissolved in acetone to an aqueous phase under stirring leads to the formation of colloidal particles. The mechanism of particle formation is explained by interfacial turbulences between the two liquid phases which are gov- erned by the Marangoni effect.(16) Accordingly, the originally elliptic-shaped structure of the injected solvent layer breaks down into small droplets, which again break down into smaller and smaller parts until non divisible polymer aggre- gates are left, thereby allowing reproducible production of polymeric nanoparticles in a size range from 100 to 200 nm with narrow size distributions (Table 1 and Fig. 2). The usefulness of the solvent displacement technique is often limited to lipophilic drugs that are highly soluble in polar organic solvents, but only slightly soluble in water, to avoid extensive loss of the drug into the aqueous phase FIG. 4.
