PULMONARY TARGETING WITH SALBUTAMOL-LOADED NANOPARTICLES 53

during nanoparticle formation. Encapsulation of water- soluble drugs is a difﬁcult task, due to the low afﬁnity of the drug compounds to the polymers on the one hand, and the short distances drugs have to cover to diffuse out of the particles on the other hand.(21) Thus, poor incorporation of the low molecular weight hydrophilic drug salbutamol into nanoparticles prepared from the hydrophobic polymer PLGA was observed (Table 1). The gained encapsulation efﬁciency was in good agreement with previous reports.(22,23) The solvent selection and addition of different excipients to the inner organic phase, like surfactants, was found to im- prove encapsulation efﬁciency. The same results were made by manipulating the outer water phase by pH changes or addition of salts.(24,25) However, these methods were not considered for the preparation of salbutamol-loaded nano- particles by the solvent displacement technique used in this study. Our approach included the synthesis of a novel class of biodegradable branched polyesters suitable for nano- particle production.(11) The introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure would promote electrostatic interactions with oppositely charged drugs, thereby improving the design of nanoparti- culate carriers as drug delivery systems to the lung. Surface characterization studies of salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles suggested that both nanoparticle formulations exhibited a negative surface charge, displayed by a negative z-potential (Table 1). An increased negative surface charge was detected for salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles prepared from VS(72)-10 due to the introduction of negatively charged functional groups inside the polymer structure. It has been previ- ously reported that small molecules can be complexed and encapsulated efﬁciently via electrostatic interactions with polyelectrolytes.(26) This concept is also proposed for the salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. Vigorously mix- ing of salbutamol together with the VS(72)-10 polymer prior to the nanoparticle preparation process ensued a high sal- butamol encapsulation efﬁciency (Table 1). In vitro release studies Drug release from pulmonary drug delivery systems often depends on the used delivery system as well as on the physicochemical properties of the drug. Therefore, the in vitro release proﬁles of the salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle prep- arations were investigated in PBS pH 7.4. Freshly prepared salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles showed no retarda- tion of drug release, whereas for freshly prepared salbuta- mol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles a sustained release over 1 to 2 h was observed. Neither a difference in curve pro- gression nor a difference in the released amount of salbuta- mol was seen after nebulization for both formulations. Both nanoparticle formulations released the whole encapsulated amount of salbutamol during incubation time (Fig. 3). The release rates of drug compounds from nanoparticulate drug delivery systems is thought to be dependent upon desorp- tion of the surfacebound, adsorbed drug, diffusion through the nanoparticle matrix, rate of polymer degradation, and a combined process of degradation and diffusion. Thus, dif- fusion and biodegradation govern the process of drug re- lease.(27) We suppose that salbutamol release from PLGA nanoparticles was attributed to the low afﬁnity of the drug to the polymer,(22,23) as also indicated by the low encapsulation efﬁciency. Dilution of the nanoparticle suspensions with an excess of PBS at pH 7.4 favored diffusion of the encap- sulated drug into the release medium. In a previous study, Lai et al.(2) have prepared PLGA microparticles loaded with isoproterenol (mean diameter: 4.5 mm) for sustained bron- chodilation experiments in rats. Upon dilution with instilla- tion medium a large burst release was found, despite a longer distance the drug had to cover to diffuse out of the microparticles. The particles released about 70% of the in- corporated drug within 5 min, displaying the low afﬁnity of the base isoproterenol to the PLGA matrix. An introduction of negatively charged functional groups in the structure of the VS(72)-10 polymer promoted electrostatic interactions between the polymeric nanoparticles and the oppositely charged salbutamol, resulting in higher encapsulation efﬁ- ciency and sustained release of salbutamol. Nanoparticle stability during nebulization Among the large number of synthetic polymers available for the preparation of polymeric nanoparticles, the most commonly used belong to the family of polyesters. These polymers are known to exhibit excellent biocompatibility and adequate biodegradation under physiological conditions. However, linear polyesters like PLGA have many limitations as nanoparticulate drug delivery systems to the lungs. First, PLGA nanoparticles degrade over a period of weeks to months, but typically deliver drugs for a shorter period of time.(8) Such a pattern would lead to an unwanted accumu- lation of polymer in the lungs when repeated administrations are needed. Second, PLGA nanoparticles have hydrophobic surfaces, which result in particle aggregation upon nebuliza- tion.(28) Branched polyesters like VS(72)-10 show signiﬁcant potential to overcome the drawbacks of linear polyesters. Due to their unique properties, such as biocompatibility and con- trolled variability of biodegradation rates, they are highly suited for pulmonary application. Through polymer modiﬁ- cation biodegradation rates may be manipulated to achieve half-lives ranging from several hours to several weeks.(9,11) Further, their amphiphilic character and the resulting in- crease in hydrophilicity provide advantages when nebuliza- tion is required. In this study the MicroSprayer was used for nebulization of the nanoparticle suspensions. The investiga- tion of nanoparticle formulation stability to the aerosolization process ensued that salbutamol-loaded PLGA nanoparticles were prone to aggregation during the process of nebulization, displayed by a signiﬁcant increase in particle size and PDI ( p < 0.05), whereas z-potential and encapsulation efﬁciency were not signiﬁcantly affected by nebulization. For salbuta- mol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles only a negligible change in particle size, z-potential, and encapsulation efﬁciency was detected. Although statistically signiﬁcant, the difference in the polydispersity index before and after nebulization was smaller when compared with the difference for salbutamol- loaded PLGA nanoparticles (Table 2). The size measurements gained by PCS were in good agreement with the AFM data (Fig. 2). MicroSprayer characterization In this study the MicroSprayer was used for intratracheal aerosol application to the IPL. The aerosol delivery was syn- chronized with the inspiration phase and the dose was 54 BECK-BROICHSITTER ET AL.

delivered when approximately 10 to 20% of the tidal volume had been inhaled. The aerosol deposition in isolated, per- fused, and ventilated lung models has been demonstrated to be inversely proportional to the respiratory frequency.(29) Accordingly, the breathing frequency in the IPL experiments was decreased from 30 to 6 strokes=min during nebulization, resulting in a longer inhalation period with lower ﬂow rate, to facilitate a peripheral lung deposition. Tronde et al.(30) have used a similar method, an intratracheal nebulization catheter technique synchronized with inspiration, for the delivery of aerosols to small rodents and isolated rat lungs. In the isolated and perfused lung, they achieved high total aerosol deposi- tion (*100%) and demonstrated that about 40% of the de- posited aerosol dose was distributed to the parenchymal region. Beck et al.(31) investigated the regional deposition of aerosols delivered to the lungs of monkeys by means of a MicroSprayer. Analysis of their data demonstrates a pe- ripheral lung deposition of approximately 40%. Because the aerodynamic properties, displayed by the MMAD and GSD, are important parameters for aerosol deposition within the lungs, the size distribution of the formulation aerosols was determined by laser diffraction. This method is well estab- lished for size analysis of aerosols, and has shown good cor- relation with pulmonary deposition ﬁndings.(32) The MMAD and GSD of aerosolized saline and all salbutamol formula- tions in this study were in the range of previously reported magnitudes for other liquids.(14,31) Therefore, a total aerosol deposition of 100% of the nebulized salbutamol formulations with a signiﬁcant delivery to peripheral lung regions can be expected when intratracheally nebulized and synchronized with inspiration in the isolated rabbit lung. Pulmonary absorption and distribution characteristics of nebulized salbutamol formulations in the IPL The promising in vitro results led us to the examination of both salbutamol-loaded nanoparticle formulations as po- tential pulmonary drug delivery systems in separate IPL experiments. Therefore, the pulmonary absorption and dis- tribution characteristics of salbutamol after aerosolization as solution or entrapped into nanoparticles were veriﬁed. As expected from previous investigations demonstrating good biocompatibility of intrapulmonary administered polymeric nanoparticles,(33) the isolated lungs fulﬁlled the three claimed criteria after nebulization of the salbutamol-loaded nano- particles. During the whole experiment they maintained homogeneous white appearance with no signs of hemostasis, edema, or atelectasis; a pulmonary artery and ventilation pressure in the normal range; and isogravimetry. The sal- butamol absorption from the air space into the perfusate during the IPL experiments was characterized by two dis- tinct phases for all formulations (Table 2 and Fig. 4). The initial continuous increase of the transferred salbutamol into the perfusate was followed by a slower second absorption phase. A similar pulmonary absorption proﬁle has been reported previously for the hydrophilic ﬂuorescence dye 6- carboxyﬂuorescein in rats.(34) It was proposed that the 6- carboxyﬂuorescein absorption proﬁle from the lung after intratracheal instillation was incongruous with a simple ﬁrst- order input. Therefore, they suggested an absorption model involving two simultaneous ﬁrst-order absorption processes by both active transport and passage through the pores of the epithelium membrane. The drug absorption proﬁle dur- ing the initial phase from salbutamol-loaded nanoparticles hardly differed from those of salbutamol solution, as indi- cated by similar apparent ﬁrst-order fast absorption rate constants ka (Table 3). Hence, during the initial phase of drug absorption by the IPL the unencapsulated salbutamol from both nanoparticle formulations (75 and 40% for PLGA and VS(72)-10, respectively; Tables 1 and 2) appeared in the perfusate. The following second absorption phase disclosed differences between the three formulations. Only negligible differences were seen for salbutamol solution and salbutamol- loaded PLGA nanoparticles, as expected by the low encap- sulation efﬁciency and fast drug release in the in vitro studies. Signiﬁcant slower drug absorption from salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles compared to salbutamol solution was observed and added up to a retention of salbutamol in the lung tissue, thereby providing a sustained pharmaco- logical effect (Table 3 and Fig. 4). The slower drug absorption phase for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles corre- sponds to the sustained drug release in vitro. Regarding the lung architecture, there are only small amounts of liquid at the surface of the respiratory tract. Thus, the reduced dilu- tion effect in the lung after aerosolization of the VS(72)-10 nanoparticle suspensions results in an even more pro- nounced retard effect compared to the in vitro situation. However, it should be appreciated that the biexponential method used for analysis of the IPL perfusate data can produce misleading results. The values for the slow ab- sorption rate constant, kb, in particular are very susceptible to errors due to (1) small changes in the amounts retained within the formulation in vivo and (2) changes in regional deposition that may occur between formulations. The IPL preparation as used studies absorption into the pulmonary circulation; the bronchial circulation is severed, and drug substance depositing in the upper airways may be unavail- able for absorption into the perfusate, irrespective of its physical form. The gained absorption proﬁle and signiﬁcantly decreased total recovery for the salbutamol from VS(72)-10 nano- particles (49%) compared to the total recovery for salbutamol from solution (75%) (Fig. 5), might have been attributed to internalization of salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles into lung cells. In fact, Dailey et al.(8) demonstrated that PLGA-derived nanoparticles were taken up by A549 cells. Assuming the localization of VS(72)-10 nanoparticles in the interstitial spaces, dilution of the nanoparticle suspension would have led to a release of encapsulated salbutamol with consequent rapid absorption into the perfusate. Additionally, an increased amount of salbutamol would have been found in the lavage ﬂuid. But this was not the case. Thus, studies on the localization of the nanoparticles in lung cells and in dif- ferent lung compartments after aerosol application are indi- cated to fully explain the absorption and distribution patterns reported in this study. Conclusion In conclusion, the results of this study highlight the po- tential of colloidal pulmonary drug delivery systems for the treatment of respiratory diseases. Polymeric nanoparticles were prepared from two different biodegradable polyes- ters by a solvent displacement method. High encapsulation PULMONARY TARGETING WITH SALBUTAMOL-LOADED NANOPARTICLES 55

efﬁciencies for salbutamol were only achieved using the bio- compatible, branched polyester VS(72)-10. In vitro release studies showed a sustained release of the encapsulated sal- butamol from the VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. Investigation of nanoparticle formulation stability to the aerosolization process utilizing the MicroSprayer ensured that the characteristics and encapsulation efﬁciencies were only negligibly affected by nebulization for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles. After inhalative administration of salbutamol formulations to the IPL, salbu- tamol showed a reproducible pulmonary absorption pro- ﬁle characterized by two distinct phases for all formulations. The initial drug absorption phase exhibited superimposed curves, whereas the second absorption phase disclosed slower absorption behavior for salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles compared to salbutamol solution. Thus, the absorption degree of salbutamol released from VS(72)-10 na- noparticles by the IPL was less than those of salbutamol from solution denoted by a lower total recovery. The in vitro release proﬁle was in agreement with the ex vivo absorption proﬁle. The amount of salbutamol that remained in the other com- partments of the IPL was similar for VS(72)-10 nanoparticles and solution indicating an uptake of the salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles into pulmonary cells. In summary, biocompatible salbutamol-loaded VS(72)-10 nanoparticles demonstrated a pulmonary sustained drug release after in- halative administration. The results from this ex vivo study will hopefully promote progress in nanomedicine.
